elements.subsection-2411-006: A Toy Reduction for Block-wise Sparsity
elements.sent-2411-166: A gadget mapping dense $L$-dimensional vectors to {\em blockwise $k$-sparse $n$-dimensional vectors is not hard to construct.}
elements.sent-2411-167: A blockwise sparse vector is one whose coordinates are divided into $k$ blocks, each of which has a single non-zero coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-168: Indeed, for a parameter $t=L/k$, let $H \in (\zqz)^{t \times q^{t }$ denote a matrix whose columns are all the vectors in $(\zqz)^{t}$.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Then, we will let the decoding gadget $G = I_k \otimes H \in (\zqz)^{t k \times q^{t k}$.}
elements.sent-2411-170: First of all, since $L=t k$ and $n= q^{t k$, notice that \begin{equation}L = \frac{k\log (n/k)}{\log q} \,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-171: Secondly, given an $L$-dimensional vector $b$, one can decode each block of $t$ coordinates of $b$ into a unit vector in $q^{t}$ dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-172: Concatenating the unit vectors gives us a block-wise sparse $n$-dimensional vector $a$ such that $a^\top G = b^\top$.
elements.sent-2411-173: Permuting the coordinates might seem to suffice to turn blockwise sparsity into true sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-174: However, since the same permutation has to be used for all $m$ samples, the blockwise structure starts becoming apparent from even a small number of samples.
elements.sent-2411-175: For example, given $\omega(n^2)$ samples, every two coordinates $i,j$ appear in some row of the public matrix $A$ when the supports are uniformly random $k$-tuples.
elements.sent-2411-176: This property is not shared by the blockwise model for any number of samples under any permutation.
elements.sent-2411-177: To decode to random sparse vectors, we need a cleverer idea which we describe next.
elements.sent-2411-178: We begin with an informal non-mathematical overview.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Motivation for Our Reduction: Translation of Natural Languages
elements.sent-2411-179: The problem at hand is reminiscent of an age-old task: translating from one language (dense coefficient vectors) to another (sparse coefficient vectors).
elements.sent-2411-180: Translation of natural languages motivates our approach.
elements.sent-2411-181: First, we split each sentence (coefficient vector) into words and translate word-by-word using a dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-182: Second, we use the grammar of the two languages to combine the respective words into meaningful sentences (coefficient vectors).
elements.sent-2411-183: The intuition why such an approach is efficient is that the small number of possible words (as opposed to possible sentences or even paragraphs) allows for efficient translation.
elements.sent-2411-184: Imagine if one had to print a dictionary in the real world containing translations of all possible sentences! For us, the grammar of the ``dense language'' is simple.
elements.sent-2411-185: Each sentence is simply a concatenation of words.
elements.sent-2411-186: Our dictionary allows us to translate each of these words into a sequence of $k$ indices over some small domain.
elements.sent-2411-187: These new sequences of length $k$ are the words in the ``sparse language''.
elements.sent-2411-188: One of our contributions is to specify a grammar that allows us to combine $k$ indices over a small domain into a $k$-sparse equation.
elements.sent-2411-189: It should already be clear that the approach taken is more sophisticated than simple concatenation, which would blow-up the sparsity $k.$.
